Document [21] (Title: Brodalumab -- Indications) Phase 3 studies comparing brodalumab with ustekinumab showed that at standard dosing, brodalumab was able to achieve higher rates of PASI 100, 44%, and 37% versus 22% and 19% of patients treated with ustekinumab. Brodalumab is also more effective than ustekinumab in biologic-experienced patients: 40% of patients previously treated with a biologic medication achieved PASI 100, while only 17% of biologic-experienced patients treated with ustekinumab were able to achieve the same level of efficacy. Additionally, brodalumab had a more rapid onset of action with PASI 75 responders reaching this benchmark in a median of 4.2 weeks compared to 9.4 weeks for ustekinumab. [4] [5]

Document [27] (Title: Tocilizumab -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses) SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) - Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) The Infectious Diseases Society of America recommends treatment with tocilizumab and a glucocorticoid (eg, dexamethasone) in hospitalized adults with declining blood oxygen saturation levels (eg, pulse oximetry ≤94%) and patients on mechanical ventilation or oxygenation with signs of increased systemic inflammation (eg, C-reactive protein). The National Institutes of Health recommends treatment with tocilizumab and a glucocorticoid (eg, dexamethasone) in hospitalized patients with rapidly declining pulmonary function. For example, hospitalized patients who are candidates for intensive care unit (ICU) admission and require mechanical ventilation or oxygenation or hospitalized patients with rapidly declining pulmonary function requiring ventilation or oxygenation with signs of increased systemic inflammation. A clinical trial by Gordon et al observed that ICU COVID-19 pneumonia patients had improved clinical outcomes, including survival with tocilizumab treatment. [4] A clinical trial by Salama C. et al observed that in hospitalized COVID-19 pneumonia patients who were not on mechanical ventilation, tocilizumab treatment reduced the risk of progression to mechanical ventilation or death. However, the drug did not increase patient survival. [5]

Document [4] (Title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 2 -- Community 690) Fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) group “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases.” This categorisation indicates that each of these agents is used as a systemic therapy for conditions characterized by airway obstruction, such as asthma or chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, when inhaled treatments are insufficient. Although they share a common therapeutic class, the drugs have distinct mechanisms of action: fenspiride is a non‑steroidal anti‑inflammatory and bronchodilator; benralizumab, mepolizumab and reslizumab are monoclonal antibodies that target the interleukin‑5 pathway to reduce eosinophilic inflammation; and tezepelumab is a monoclonal antibody that blocks thymic stromal lymphopoietin (TSLP), a key upstream cytokine in airway inflammation. Their inclusion in the same ATC subgroup underscores their role as systemic options for managing obstructive airway diseases, guiding clinicians in selecting appropriate non‑inhaled treatments for patients with severe or refractory disease.

Document [23] (Title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 416) Amlexanox, fenspiride, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are all classified within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) system as drugs for obstructive airway diseases. Within this broad category, the ATC hierarchy defines two specialized subgroups: “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” and “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants.” The subgroup “other systemic drugs for obstructive airway diseases” is an inverse‑isa of the parent class “DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES,” indicating that it is a more specific category under the overall group. The same inverse‑isa relationship exists for the inhalant subgroup, and the inhalant subgroup is also explicitly linked to the parent class by an isa relationship, confirming its status as a direct child of the broader category. Amlexanox, benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab are members of the systemic subgroup, reflecting that they are administered systemically (e.g., oral, subcutaneous or intravenous routes) for the treatment of conditions such as asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and other obstructive airway disorders. Fenspiride is listed as a member of both the systemic subgroup and the inhalant subgroup, suggesting that it may be used either systemically or as an inhaled preparation in the management of obstructive airway disease. The inclusion of biologic agents such as benralizumab, mepolizumab, reslizumab and tezepelumab highlights the role of targeted monoclonal‑antibody therapies in severe asthma and related airway inflammation, while amlexanox’s placement in the systemic subgroup points to its potential off‑label or investigational use for airway disease. This ATC classification framework clarifies the therapeutic positioning of each drug, aids clinicians in selecting appropriate treatment routes, and supports consistent pharmacovigilance and prescribing practices for patients with obstructive airway diseases.
